# AI Session Transcripts

These are curated summaries of AI-assisted development sessions for ReviewLens AI. Each document captures the key decisions, trade-offs, dead ends, and outcomes from working with Claude Code.

## Sessions

| Session | Focus | Key Outcome |
|---------|-------|-------------|
| [01 - Architecture](session-01-architecture.md) | Project scaffolding & stack decisions | Next.js 16 + Bedrock + Comprehend architecture |
| [02 - Scraping](session-02-scraping-and-ingestion.md) | Ingestion pipeline & Apify integration | Replaced SDK with REST API to fix Turbopack compatibility |
| [03 - Streaming & UI](session-03-streaming-and-ui.md) | Real-time chat & glassmorphism polish | Pull-based SSE streaming, chat drawer, markdown rendering |
| [04 - AWS Integration](session-04-aws-integration.md) | Comprehend, Bedrock, IAM permissions | Dual-layer guardrails, graceful fallbacks |

## AI Tool Used
- **Claude Code** (CLI) powered by Claude Opus 4.6
- Used for: architecture decisions, code generation, debugging, AWS integration, UI implementation

## Philosophy
AI was used as a force multiplier — not a replacement for engineering judgment. Key architectural decisions (scope guard strategy, fallback chains, data flow) were driven by product thinking; AI accelerated the implementation.

Perfect! Here are all 4 session transcripts with their full contents:

## Session 1 — Architecture & Project Scaffolding (2026-03-18)
**Duration:** ~45 min | **AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)

**Objective:** Design and scaffold the ReviewLens AI project — a review intelligence portal with guardrailed Q&A.

**Key Decisions:**
- Stack: Next.js 16 (App Router) + Amazon Bedrock (Claude via ConverseStream API) + Amazon Comprehend + Apify for scraping
- Architecture: Ingest → Scrape/Parse → Comprehend Sentiment → In-memory session store → Bedrock streaming Q&A
- Guardrails: Dual-layer (system prompt + Bedrock Guardrails) for scope enforcement
- Trade-off: In-memory store vs DynamoDB — chose in-memory for zero-cost POC

**What Went Well:** AI quickly generated clean project structure; good component breakdown from the start.

**Dead Ends:** Puppeteer Lambda scraping deferred for faster iteration.

---

## Session 2 — Scraping & Ingestion Pipeline (2026-03-18)
**Duration:** ~30 min | **AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)

**Objective:** Build review ingestion pipeline — URL scraping via Apify and CSV upload fallback.

**Key Decisions:**
- **Apify Integration:** Replaced `apify-client` SDK with direct REST API calls using `fetch` to eliminate Turbopack MODULE_NOT_FOUND errors (removed 40 packages)
- **Overall Rating:** Scrape product page HTML in parallel to extract real overall rating and total global ratings count (not just computed from sample)
- **CSV Parser:** Generated robust parser handling quoted fields, various column name mappings, edge cases

**What Went Well:** Parallel fetching kept ingestion fast; fallback chain (scrape → CSV) ensures reliability.

**Dead Ends:** Lambda/Puppeteer scraper removed — added complexity without POC value.

---

## Session 3 — Streaming Chat & UI Polish (2026-03-18)
**Duration:** ~60 min | **AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)

**Objective:** Implement real-time streaming Q&A chat with Bedrock and polish glassmorphism UI.

**Key Decisions:**
- **Chat Drawer:** Sliding panel from the right (85vw tablet → 65vw desktop) with backdrop blur, ESC to close
- **Streaming Architecture:** Fixed chunking issues by:
  - Switching to `pull()` pattern per Next.js 16 docs (iterator-to-stream)
  - Set `compress: false` in next.config.ts
  - Added `Accept-Encoding: identity` on client fetch
  - Added `export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"` to chat route
- **Turbopack Worker Isolation:** Return full session data from ingest API, store in sessionStorage, pass to chat route for hydration
- **Markdown Rendering:** Added `react-markdown` with custom CSS matching glassmorphism design

**UI Enhancements:** Sentiment-colored review cards, confidence badges, white tooltip text, responsive drawer width

**What Went Well:** Pull-based streaming was correct approach; chat drawer UX much better than separate page.

**Dead Ends:** TransformStream (still buffered), ScrollArea from shadcn (ref issues), artificial token queue animation.

---

## Session 4 — AWS Integration & Permissions (2026-03-18)
**Duration:** ~20 min | **AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)

**Objective:** Wire up Amazon Comprehend for sentiment analysis and Amazon Bedrock for Q&A, resolve IAM permissions.

**Key Decisions:**
- **Comprehend:** Added IAM inline policy with `comprehend:BatchDetectSentiment`; rating-based fallback keeps app working
- **Bedrock Guardrails:** Configured with dual-layer approach (system prompt + Bedrock Guardrails API)
- **Bedrock Streaming:** Using inference profile ID (not raw model ID), `maxTokens: 1024`, `temperature: 0.3`, proper event handling for contentBlockDelta and assessments

**What Went Well:** Comprehend fallback enabled graceful degradation; dual-layer guardrails provide strong scope enforcement.

---

**Format Pattern:** Each session follows Problem → Decision → Implementation → What Went Well / Dead Ends structure, focusing on AI-assisted decision points, technical trade-offs, and learnings. Topics span architecture, scraping/ingestion, real-time streaming, UI/UX, and AWS service integration.

# Session 06 — Multi-Platform Ingestion, Testing & Branding (2026-03-19)

**Duration:** ~45 min | **AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)

## Objective
Expand the ingestion pipeline to support Google Maps and Yelp alongside Amazon, add unit tests with Vitest, create custom branding assets, and produce a design book.

## Prompts (summarised)
1. "Extend the ingestion form to support multiple platforms — Amazon, Google Maps, Yelp, and a generic CSV-only 'Other' option. Each platform should show its own URL format, help text, and instructions"
2. "Set up Vitest and write unit tests for the analytics module, CSV parser, prompt builder, and session store"
3. "Create a DESIGN_BOOK.md documenting the UI design system — colours, typography, glassmorphism effects, component patterns"
4. "Design custom SVG logos and icons for ReviewLens branding, replace the default Next.js/Vercel assets"
5. "Remove the Lambda/Puppeteer scraper code — we're not using it in the POC"

## Key Decisions
- **Platform Config Map:** Created a `PLATFORM_CONFIG` record mapping each platform to its URL pattern, placeholder, help text, and step-by-step instructions. The `Other` platform disables URL scraping and only allows CSV upload.
- **Enhanced CSV Parser:** Rewrote the parser to handle a wider variety of column name mappings, quoted fields, and edge cases across platforms — grew from ~53 to ~426 lines.
- **Scraper Multi-Platform:** Extended `scraper.ts` to detect platform from URL and route to the appropriate Apify actor (Amazon, Google Maps, Yelp).
- **Vitest Setup:** Added `vitest`, `@testing-library/react`, `@testing-library/jest-dom`. Created `vitest.config.mts` with path aliases. Wrote 4 test suites covering analytics, CSV parsing, prompt construction, and store operations.
- **Lambda Removal:** Deleted `lambda/` directory and `scripts/deploy-lambda.sh` — Puppeteer scraping was deferred in Session 01 and never used.
- **Custom Branding:** Created `icon.svg`, `logo.svg`, and `logo-text.svg`; updated Header component with new branding. Replaced default `favicon.ico` with custom SVG icon.
- **DESIGN_BOOK.md:** Documented the full design system — colour palette, glassmorphism parameters, animation specs, component anatomy.

## What Went Well
- Platform config pattern made the IngestForm cleanly extensible without branching logic.
- Vitest integrated smoothly with Next.js 16 and the existing TypeScript/path-alias setup.
- Removing Lambda code simplified the project and eliminated unused deployment scripts.

## Dead Ends
- Yelp URL scraping is limited by Apify actor availability — falls back to CSV for now.

# Session 07 — Architecture Diagrams & Vision-Based Scraping (2026-03-19)

**Duration:** ~15 min | **AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)

## Objective
Create comprehensive architecture diagrams for the project documentation and document a vision-based scraping approach as a future improvement.

## Prompts (summarised)
1. "Create draw.io architecture diagrams covering: overall app architecture, cloud architecture, frontend structure, backend structure, and user flow"
2. "Add a section to FUTURE_IMPROVEMENTS.md about vision-based scraping — using screenshots + AI vision models instead of DOM parsing, and how it could work as a browser extension"

## Key Decisions
- **Five Diagrams:** Created `.drawio` files in `docs/diagrams/` covering:
  - `app-architecture.drawio` — end-to-end system overview (client → Next.js → AWS services)
  - `cloud-architecture.drawio` — AWS service topology (Amplify, Bedrock, Comprehend, Apify)
  - `frontend-structure.drawio` — component tree and page hierarchy
  - `backend-structure.drawio` — API routes, lib modules, data flow
  - `user-flow.drawio` — user journey from URL input through analysis to chat
- **Vision-Based Scraping Concept:** Documented an alternative scraping approach that captures page screenshots and uses AI vision models (e.g., Claude's vision capability) to extract review data — more resilient to DOM changes than traditional selectors. Outlined potential implementation as a browser extension.

## What Went Well

1→# Session 1 — Architecture & Project Scaffolding
     2→**Date:** 2026-03-18
     3→**AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)
     4→**Duration:** ~45 min
     5→
     6→## Objective
     7→Design and scaffold the ReviewLens AI project — a review intelligence portal with guardrailed Q&A.
     8→
     9→## Key Decisions Made with AI
    10→
    11→### Stack Selection
    12→- **Prompt:** "Build a review intelligence portal using AWS services, Next.js, and keep costs at zero."
    13→- **Decision:** Next.js 16 (App Router) + Amazon Bedrock (Claude via ConverseStream API) + Amazon Comprehend + Apify for scraping
    14→- **Rationale:** Leverages existing AWS credentials, free-tier Comprehend, Bedrock pay-per-token model
    15→
    16→### Architecture
    17→- **Prompt:** "How should I structure the data flow from ingestion to Q&A?"
    18→- **Decision:** Ingest → Scrape/Parse → Comprehend Sentiment → In-memory session store → Bedrock streaming Q&A
    19→- **Trade-off discussed:** In-memory store vs DynamoDB — chose in-memory for zero-cost POC, noted DynamoDB as production path
    20→
    21→### Guardrail Strategy
    22→- **Prompt:** "I need dual-layer scope guard — system prompt + Bedrock Guardrails"
    23→- **Decision:** System prompt enforces review-only scope with decline templates; Bedrock Guardrails adds topic-level blocking as second layer
    24→- **AI helped:** Draft the system prompt with strict rules, decline templates, and review citation requirements
    25→
    26→## What Went Well
    27→- AI quickly generated the project structure following Next.js 16 conventions
    28→- Component breakdown (IngestForm, StatsCards, ChatInterface, etc.) was clean from the start
    29→
    30→## Dead Ends
    31→- Initially considered Puppeteer Lambda for scraping — decided to defer and use Apify first for faster iteration
    32→

1→# Session 2 — Scraping & Ingestion Pipeline
     2→**Date:** 2026-03-18
     3→**AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)
     4→**Duration:** ~30 min
     5→
     6→## Objective
     7→Build the review ingestion pipeline — URL scraping via Apify and CSV upload fallback.
     8→
     9→## Key Decisions Made with AI
    10→
    11→### Apify Integration
    12→- **Problem:** `apify-client` SDK caused `MODULE_NOT_FOUND` errors under Turbopack due to dynamic `require()` calls
    13→- **AI suggested:** Replace SDK with direct REST API calls using `fetch` — eliminated the dynamic import issue entirely
    14→- **Result:** Removed `apify-client` dependency (40 packages), zero Turbopack conflicts
    15→
    16→### Overall Rating Scraping
    17→- **Problem:** App showed "Average Rating 4.9/5" computed from scraped sample, but Amazon shows "4.7/5" from 27,405 ratings
    18→- **Decision:** Scrape the product page HTML in parallel with review scraping to extract the real overall rating and total global ratings count
    19→- **Implementation:** Lightweight `fetch` of the product page, regex extraction of "X out of 5 stars" and "N global ratings"
    20→
    21→### CSV Parser
    22→- **AI helped:** Generate a robust CSV parser handling quoted fields, various column name mappings (rating/stars, body/text/review), and edge cases
    23→
    24→## What Went Well
    25→- Parallel fetching (overall rating + Apify reviews) kept ingestion fast
    26→- Fallback chain (scrape → CSV) gives users a reliable path regardless of anti-bot measures
    27→
    28→## Dead Ends
    29→- Lambda/Puppeteer scraper was scaffolded but removed — added complexity without value for the POC timeline
    30→

1→# Session 3 — Streaming Chat & UI Polish
     2→**Date:** 2026-03-18
     3→**AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)
     4→**Duration:** ~60 min
     5→
     6→## Objective
     7→Implement real-time streaming Q&A chat with Bedrock and polish the glassmorphism UI.
     8→
     9→## Key Decisions Made with AI
    10→
    11→### Chat as Drawer vs Full Page
    12→- **Prompt:** "Open the chat as a sliding panel rather than navigating to a separate page"
    13→- **Decision:** Built a `ChatDrawer` component — slides in from the right, responsive width (85vw tablet → 65vw desktop), backdrop blur, ESC to close
    14→- **Added:** Floating chat button on dashboard for quick access
    15→
    16→### Streaming Architecture
    17→- **Problem:** SSE streaming was arriving as 1-2 large chunks instead of token-by-token
    18→- **Root causes identified:**
    19→  1. `ReadableStream` with `start()` buffers entire response before sending
    20→  2. Next.js compression (`gzip`) buffers chunks before flushing
    21→  3. Browser `Accept-Encoding` header causes response buffering
    22→- **Fixes applied:**
    23→  - Switched to `pull()` pattern per Next.js 16 docs (iterator-to-stream)
    24→  - Set `compress: false` in next.config.ts
    25→  - Added `Accept-Encoding: identity` on client fetch
    26→  - Added `export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"` to chat route
    27→
    28→### Turbopack Worker Isolation
    29→- **Problem:** In-memory session store was invisible across Turbopack workers — dashboard got 404s, chat got "session not found"
    30→- **Fix:** Return full session data from ingest API, store in sessionStorage, pass to chat route on first message for hydration
    31→
    32→### Markdown Rendering
    33→- **Problem:** Bedrock responses contain markdown (headings, bold, lists) but rendered as plain text
    34→- **Fix:** Added `react-markdown` with custom CSS theme matching the glassmorphism design
    35→
    36→## UI Enhancements
    37→- Sentiment-colored left borders on review cards (green/red/amber/slate)
    38→- Confidence score percentage badges
    39→- White tooltip text in charts (was black on dark background)
    40→- Responsive chat drawer width using viewport units
    41→
    42→## What Went Well
    43→- Pull-based streaming pattern from Next.js 16 docs was the correct approach
    44→- Chat drawer UX is much better than a separate page — users stay in context
    45→
    46→## Dead Ends
    47→- `TransformStream` approach — still buffered in Turbopack dev
    48→- `ScrollArea` from shadcn — ref pointed to wrong element, replaced with plain overflow div
    49→- Artificial token queue animation — rejected in favor of real streaming behavior
    50→

1→# Session 4 — AWS Integration & Permissions
     2→**Date:** 2026-03-18
     3→**AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)
     4→**Duration:** ~20 min
     5→
     6→## Objective
     7→Wire up Amazon Comprehend for sentiment analysis and Amazon Bedrock for Q&A, resolve IAM permissions.
     8→
     9→## Key Decisions Made with AI
    10→
    11→### Comprehend Sentiment Analysis
    12→- **Problem:** `AccessDeniedException` — IAM user lacked `comprehend:BatchDetectSentiment` permission
    13→- **Fix:** Created inline IAM policy via AWS Console with minimal permission scope
    14→- **Fallback:** Rating-based sentiment (≥4 stars = positive, ≤2 = negative) already in place — app works even without Comprehend
    15→
    16→### Bedrock Guardrails
    17→- **Setup:** Configured `BEDROCK_GUARDRAIL_ID` and `BEDROCK_GUARDRAIL_VERSION` in .env
    18→- **Dual-layer approach:**
    19→  1. System prompt with strict scope rules and decline templates
    20→  2. Bedrock Guardrails with topic policies for out-of-scope blocking
    21→- **AI helped:** Structure the guardrail error handling — catch both stream-level guardrail events and thrown exceptions
    22→
    23→### Bedrock Streaming
    24→- **Model:** Using inference profile ID (not raw model ID) for on-demand access
    25→- **Config:** `maxTokens: 1024`, `temperature: 0.3` for focused analytical responses
    26→- **AI helped:** Implement the `ConverseStreamCommand` async generator with proper event handling for `contentBlockDelta`, `inputAssessment`, and `outputAssessments`
    27→
    28→## What Went Well
    29→- Comprehend fallback meant the app never broke — just degraded gracefully
    30→- Guardrail dual-layer gives strong scope enforcement without being brittle
    31→

# Session 3 — Streaming Chat & UI Polish
**Date:** 2026-03-18
**AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)
**Duration:** ~60 min

## Objective
Implement real-time streaming Q&A chat with Bedrock and polish the glassmorphism UI.

## Key Decisions Made with AI

### Chat as Drawer vs Full Page
- **Prompt:** "Open the chat as a sliding panel rather than navigating to a separate page"
- **Decision:** Built a `ChatDrawer` component — slides in from the right, responsive width (85vw tablet → 65vw desktop), backdrop blur, ESC to close
- **Added:** Floating chat button on dashboard for quick access

### Streaming Architecture
- **Problem:** SSE streaming was arriving as 1-2 large chunks instead of token-by-token
- **Root causes identified:**
  1. `ReadableStream` with `start()` buffers entire response before sending
  2. Next.js compression (`gzip`) buffers chunks before flushing
  3. Browser `Accept-Encoding` header causes response buffering
- **Fixes applied:**
  - Switched to `pull()` pattern per Next.js 16 docs (iterator-to-stream)
  - Set `compress: false` in next.config.ts
  - Added `Accept-Encoding: identity` on client fetch
  - Added `export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"` to chat route

### Turbopack Worker Isolation
- **Problem:** In-memory session store was invisible across Turbopack workers — dashboard got 404s, chat got "session not found"
- **Fix:** Return full session data from ingest API, store in sessionStorage, pass to chat route on first message for hydration

### Markdown Rendering
- **Problem:** Bedrock responses contain markdown (headings, bold, lists) but rendered as plain text
- **Fix:** Added `react-markdown` with custom CSS theme matching the glassmorphism design

## UI Enhancements
- Sentiment-colored left borders on review cards (green/red/amber/slate)
- Confidence score percentage badges
- White tooltip text in charts (was black on dark background)
- Responsive chat drawer width using viewport units

## What Went Well
- Pull-based streaming pattern from Next.js 16 docs was the correct approach
- Chat drawer UX is much better than a separate page — users stay in context

## Dead Ends
- `TransformStream` approach — still buffered in Turbopack dev
- `ScrollArea` from shadcn — ref pointed to wrong element, replaced with plain overflow div
- Artificial token queue animation — rejected in favor of real streaming behavior

# Session 5 — Deployment & Configuration Hardening
**Date:** 2026-03-18
**AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)
**Duration:** ~20 min

## Objective
Prepare the app for AWS Amplify deployment, fix missing components, and clean up dependencies and environment configuration.

## Key Decisions Made with AI

### Amplify Build Configuration
- **Prompt:** "Set up an Amplify build configuration for this Next.js app"
- **Decision:** Created `amplify.yml` with `npm ci` → `npm run build`, caching `.next/cache` and `node_modules` for faster rebuilds
- **Result:** Single-file config that works with Amplify's Next.js SSR hosting out of the box

### Missing shadcn/ui Components
- **Problem:** Initial commit omitted several shadcn/ui component files (`button`, `tabs`, `textarea`, etc.) — builds failed on import
- **Prompt:** "Add all the missing shadcn/ui components that our code imports"
- **Fix:** Added all required component files; also expanded the security/networking section in `FUTURE_IMPROVEMENTS.md`

### Environment & README Cleanup
- **Prompt:** "Clean up the .env.example and README — make the env vars match what we actually use"
- **Decision:** Revised `.env.example` to reflect actual AWS/Bedrock config keys; removed stale placeholders
- **AI helped:** Simplify README descriptions to match the current architecture accurately

### Cheerio Removal & UI Tweaks
- **Prompt:** "Remove cheerio — we're not using it. Also fix the footer/header text and make the date range in StatsCards resize when text is long"
- **Decision:** Dropped `cheerio` dependency — scraping handled entirely by Apify REST API
- **UI fix:** StatsCards now adjusts font size dynamically based on date range content length

## What Went Well
- Amplify config was straightforward — `.next` output directory worked without custom build settings
- Removing cheerio eliminated unnecessary weight from the bundle

## Dead Ends
- None — these were incremental hardening tasks

# Session 6 — Multi-Platform Ingestion, Testing & Branding
**Date:** 2026-03-19
**AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)
**Duration:** ~45 min

## Objective
Expand the ingestion pipeline to support Google Maps and Yelp alongside Amazon, add unit tests with Vitest, create custom branding assets, and produce a design book.

## Key Decisions Made with AI

### Multi-Platform Ingestion
- **Prompt:** "Extend the ingestion form to support multiple platforms — Amazon, Google Maps, Yelp, and a generic CSV-only option. Each platform should show its own URL format, help text, and instructions"
- **Decision:** Created a `PLATFORM_CONFIG` record mapping each platform to its URL pattern, placeholder, help text, and step-by-step instructions
- **Implementation:** The `Other` platform disables URL scraping and only allows CSV upload; scraper detects platform from URL and routes to the appropriate Apify actor
- **AI helped:** Rewrite the CSV parser from ~53 to ~426 lines to handle a wider variety of column name mappings, quoted fields, and edge cases across platforms

### Unit Testing with Vitest
- **Prompt:** "Set up Vitest and write unit tests for the analytics module, CSV parser, prompt builder, and session store"
- **Decision:** Added `vitest`, `@testing-library/react`, `@testing-library/jest-dom` with a `vitest.config.mts` using path aliases
- **Result:** 4 test suites covering analytics calculations, CSV parsing edge cases, prompt construction, and store CRUD operations
- **AI helped:** Generate test data fixtures including a sample Shopify headphones review CSV

### Custom Branding
- **Prompt:** "Design custom SVG logos and icons for ReviewLens branding, replace the default Next.js/Vercel assets"
- **Decision:** Created `icon.svg`, `logo.svg`, and `logo-text.svg`; updated Header component with new branding
- **Cleanup:** Removed default `favicon.ico`, `next.svg`, `vercel.svg`, and other placeholder assets

### Design Book
- **Prompt:** "Create a DESIGN_BOOK.md documenting the UI design system"
- **AI helped:** Document the full design system — colour palette, glassmorphism parameters (blur, opacity, border), animation specs, component anatomy

### Lambda Removal
- **Prompt:** "Remove the Lambda/Puppeteer scraper code — we're not using it in the POC"
- **Decision:** Deleted `lambda/` directory and `scripts/deploy-lambda.sh` — Puppeteer scraping was deferred in Session 01 and never implemented

## What Went Well
- Platform config pattern made IngestForm cleanly extensible without branching logic
- Vitest integrated smoothly with Next.js 16 and the existing TypeScript/path-alias setup
- Removing Lambda code simplified the project and eliminated unused deployment scripts

## Dead Ends
- Yelp URL scraping is limited by Apify actor availability — falls back to CSV for now

# Session 7 — Architecture Diagrams & Vision-Based Scraping
**Date:** 2026-03-19
**AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)
**Duration:** ~15 min

## Objective
Create comprehensive architecture diagrams for the project documentation and document a vision-based scraping approach as a future improvement.

## Key Decisions Made with AI

### Architecture Diagrams
- **Prompt:** "Create draw.io architecture diagrams covering: overall app architecture, cloud architecture, frontend structure, backend structure, and user flow"
- **Decision:** Created five `.drawio` files in `docs/diagrams/`:
  - `app-architecture.drawio` — end-to-end system overview (client → Next.js → AWS services)
  - `cloud-architecture.drawio` — AWS service topology (Amplify, Bedrock, Comprehend, Apify)
  - `frontend-structure.drawio` — component tree and page hierarchy
  - `backend-structure.drawio` — API routes, lib modules, data flow
  - `user-flow.drawio` — user journey from URL input through analysis to chat
- **AI helped:** Generate the draw.io XML with correct node positioning, connection routing, and styling

### Vision-Based Scraping Documentation
- **Prompt:** "Add a section to FUTURE_IMPROVEMENTS.md about vision-based scraping — using screenshots and AI vision models instead of DOM parsing, and how it could work as a browser extension"
- **Decision:** Documented an alternative scraping approach that captures page screenshots and uses AI vision models (e.g., Claude's vision capability) to extract review data
- **Rationale:** More resilient to DOM changes than traditional CSS-selector scraping; outlined potential implementation as a browser extension

## What Went Well
- draw.io XML format produced detailed, editable diagrams that render in GitHub and VS Code
- Vision-based scraping concept provides a clear upgrade path beyond fragile selector-based scraping

## Dead Ends
- None — documentation session with no implementation blockers

# Session 7 — Architecture Diagrams & Vision-Based Scraping
**Date:** 2026-03-19
**AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)
**Duration:** ~15 min

## Objective
Create comprehensive architecture diagrams for the project documentation and document a vision-based scraping approach as a future improvement.

## Key Decisions Made with AI

### Architecture Diagrams
- **Prompt:** "Create draw.io architecture diagrams covering: overall app architecture, cloud architecture, frontend structure, backend structure, and user flow"
- **Decision:** Created five `.drawio` files in `docs/diagrams/`:
  - `app-architecture.drawio` — end-to-end system overview (client → Next.js → AWS services)
  - `cloud-architecture.drawio` — AWS service topology (Amplify, Bedrock, Comprehend, Apify)
  - `frontend-structure.drawio` — component tree and page hierarchy
  - `backend-structure.drawio` — API routes, lib modules, data flow
  - `user-flow.drawio` — user journey from URL input through analysis to chat
- **AI helped:** Generate the draw.io XML with correct node positioning, connection routing, and styling

### Vision-Based Scraping Documentation
- **Prompt:** "Add a section to FUTURE_IMPROVEMENTS.md about vision-based scraping — using screenshots and AI vision models instead of DOM parsing, and how it could work as a browser extension"
- **Decision:** Documented an alternative scraping approach that captures page screenshots and uses AI vision models (e.g., Claude's vision capability) to extract review data
- **Rationale:** More resilient to DOM changes than traditional CSS-selector scraping; outlined potential implementation as a browser extension

## What Went Well
- draw.io XML format produced detailed, editable diagrams that render in GitHub and VS Code
- Vision-based scraping concept provides a clear upgrade path beyond fragile selector-based scraping

## Dead Ends
- None — documentation session with no implementation blockers

# Session 07 — Architecture Diagrams & Vision-Based Scraping (2026-03-19)

**Duration:** ~15 min | **AI Tool:** Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)

## Objective
Create comprehensive architecture diagrams for the project documentation and document a vision-based scraping approach as a future improvement.

## Prompts (summarised)
1. "Create draw.io architecture diagrams covering: overall app architecture, cloud architecture, frontend structure, backend structure, and user flow"
2. "Add a section to FUTURE_IMPROVEMENTS.md about vision-based scraping — using screenshots + AI vision models instead of DOM parsing, and how it could work as a browser extension"

## Key Decisions
- **Five Diagrams:** Created `.drawio` files in `docs/diagrams/` covering:
  - `app-architecture.drawio` — end-to-end system overview (client → Next.js → AWS services)
  - `cloud-architecture.drawio` — AWS service topology (Amplify, Bedrock, Comprehend, Apify)
  - `frontend-structure.drawio` — component tree and page hierarchy
  - `backend-structure.drawio` — API routes, lib modules, data flow
  - `user-flow.drawio` — user journey from URL input through analysis to chat
- **Vision-Based Scraping Concept:** Documented an alternative scraping approach that captures page screenshots and uses AI vision models (e.g., Claude's vision capability) to extract review data — more resilient to DOM changes than traditional selectors. Outlined potential implementation as a browser extension.

## What Went Well
- draw.io XML format allowed detailed, editable diagrams that render in GitHub and VS Code.
- Vision-based scraping concept provides a clear upgrade path beyond fragile CSS-selector scraping.

## Dead Ends
- None — documentation session with no implementation blockers.
